subject: Biology
description: Initial project proposal to develop a new evaluation metric for protein structure prediction. The goal is to create a metric that better captures the functional relevance of structural differences, moving beyond simple geometric measures like GDT_TS. The plan involves analyzing existing metrics and identifying their shortcomings on difficult cases like multi-domain proteins or protein complexes.
reason: This is the first step of the research project, establishing the initial goals and a high-level plan. No prior artifacts are required as this event marks the project's inception.

subject: Biology
description: Designed the initial methodological scheme for evaluating existing metrics and developing the new one. This includes selecting the CASP dataset as the primary benchmark, choosing GDT_TS and lDDT as baseline metrics for comparison, and outlining a preliminary mathematical form for the new metric that penalizes misalignments in known functional regions.
reason: Following the initial proposal (e_01), this event translates the abstract research goals and plan into a concrete methodological design. Creating a method_scheme is a necessary prerequisite for conducting any pilot or main experiments.

subject: Biology
description: Conducted a pilot study on a small subset of 10 protein structures from the CASP dataset, including known difficult cases. The goal was to check if the new metric, which penalizes misalignments in functional regions, provides a more intuitive ranking than GDT_TS and lDDT. Preliminary results were generated to validate the core idea.
reason: After designing the initial method (e_02), conducting a pilot experiment is a crucial step to validate the feasibility of the proposed metric before committing to large-scale computations. This step uses the method_scheme to generate the first set of experiment_results, which will inform the next phase of the project.

subject: Biology
description: Analyzed the results from the pilot study. The new metric shows promise by outperforming GDT_TS on multi-domain proteins, but it is overly sensitive to minor shifts in the annotated functional regions. The analysis concluded that the weighting scheme for functional regions needs to be revised and optimized.
reason: After generating preliminary data in the pilot experiment (e_03), a dedicated analysis step is necessary to interpret the results in the context of the project's goals (e_01). This analysis identifies the strengths and weaknesses of the current method (e_02), and summarizing these findings as a draft paper paragraph solidifies the conclusions, guiding the next iteration of method design.

subject: Biology
description: Revised the methodological scheme based on the pilot study analysis. The weighting function for functional regions was modified to use a Gaussian kernel instead of a hard cutoff, making the metric less sensitive to minor alignment shifts. This change aims to improve the robustness of the evaluation score.
reason: The previous analysis (e_04) of the pilot experiment results (e_03) identified a critical flaw in the initial method_scheme, namely its over-sensitivity. Revisiting the scheme_design is a necessary step to address this issue before proceeding to larger-scale main experiments.

subject: Biology
description: Executed the main experiments by applying the revised metric (with the Gaussian kernel weighting) to the full CASP dataset. The performance of the new metric was systematically compared against GDT_TS and lDDT across all target proteins, generating a comprehensive set of results for the main analysis.
reason: After revising the methodological scheme in e_05 to address the sensitivity issues found in the pilot study, the next logical step is to conduct large-scale main experiments. This will validate the improved metric's performance and generalizability on the full benchmark dataset, moving the project from the pilot phase to the main evaluation phase.

subject: Biology
description: Performed a comprehensive analysis of the main experiment results. The analysis confirms that the revised metric with the Gaussian kernel is more robust and consistently provides more functionally relevant scores for multi-domain proteins compared to GDT_TS and lDDT. The correlation with expert assessment on a subset of difficult targets was also found to be significantly higher.
reason: Following the execution of the main experiments (e_06), a thorough analysis is the necessary next step to interpret the large volume of data generated. This event evaluates whether the revised method (e_05) successfully achieves the project's original goals (e_01), and the findings are summarized to form the basis of the results section of the paper.

subject: Biology
description: Drafted the Methods and Results sections of the research paper. The Methods section describes the final design of the new metric, including the Gaussian kernel weighting. The Results section presents the comprehensive comparison against GDT_TS and lDDT on the full CASP dataset, highlighting the improved performance on multi-domain proteins.
reason: With the main experiments run (e_06) and a comprehensive analysis completed (e_07), the core scientific work is done. The next logical step is to begin formally documenting the project's contributions by drafting the key sections of the paper, using the final method scheme and the positive results as a foundation.

subject: Biology
description: After drafting the paper sections on single-chain proteins, a review of the initial proposal highlights the unmet goal of addressing protein complexes. The team decides to expand the project's scope to test the metric's applicability to protein-protein interfaces. The research goals and plan are updated to include a new set of experiments focused on protein docking models.
reason: The successful main experiments (e_06, e_07) and the drafting of the results (e_08) provide confidence in the core metric. This is an opportune moment to revisit and expand the initial research goals (e_01) to tackle the more challenging case of protein complexes, turning the initial success into a more comprehensive and impactful study.

subject: Biology
description: Designed a new methodological scheme to extend the evaluation metric to protein complexes. This involved selecting the CAPRI benchmark dataset for protein-protein docking, defining the interface region as the primary functional area, and adapting the scoring function to evaluate the correctness of predicted inter-chain contacts.
reason: After the project's scope was expanded to include protein complexes in the proposal revision (e_09), the next logical step is to design a specific methodology for this new challenge. This event translates the updated research goals into a concrete experimental plan, adapting the successful single-chain metric for the new domain of protein-protein interfaces.

subject: Biology
description: Conducted a pilot experiment on a small subset of protein complexes from the CAPRI dataset. The goal was to validate the adapted metric's ability to score protein-protein docking models, specifically checking if it correctly identifies near-native models based on interface accuracy. This provides an initial sanity check for the new scheme.
reason: After designing a new methodological scheme for protein complexes (e_10), a pilot experiment is the logical next step. This allows for a low-cost validation of the adapted metric on the new CAPRI dataset before committing to large-scale main experiments, mirroring the successful workflow from the single-chain part of the project (e_03).

subject: Biology
description: Analyzed the results from the pilot experiment on protein complexes. The adapted metric successfully ranked rigid-body docking models but performed poorly on targets involving significant conformational change upon binding. The analysis suggests that the metric needs to incorporate a measure of intra-protein deformation, not just inter-chain interface accuracy.
reason: After generating results from the pilot experiment on protein complexes (e_11), a detailed analysis is required to assess the performance of the adapted metric. This step identifies a key limitation of the current scheme (e_10) and provides the necessary insights to guide the next round of method refinement, preventing a premature and potentially flawed main experiment.

subject: Biology
description: Revised the scheme for evaluating protein complexes based on the pilot analysis. The metric is updated to be a composite score, combining the original inter-chain interface score with a measure of intra-chain deformation for each protein partner. This aims to correctly score complexes that involve significant conformational changes upon binding.
reason: The previous analysis (e_12) revealed that the metric for complexes (designed in e_10) failed on targets with conformational changes. Therefore, it is necessary to revise the methodological scheme to incorporate a penalty for incorrect intra-protein deformation before running more extensive experiments.

subject: Biology
description: Executed the main experiments for the protein complex evaluation. The revised composite metric, which scores both inter-chain interface and intra-chain deformation, was applied to the full CAPRI benchmark dataset. This generated a comprehensive set of results for both rigid-body and flexible docking targets.
reason: After revising the scheme for protein complexes in e_13 to handle conformational changes, the next logical step is to run the main experiments. This step validates the final composite metric on the full benchmark dataset, aiming to gather the conclusive data needed to satisfy the expanded research goals defined in e_09.

subject: Biology
description: Conducted a detailed analysis of the main experiments on protein complexes. The results show that the composite metric significantly outperforms standard docking metrics like DockQ, especially for targets involving flexible binding. The analysis validates that combining inter-chain and intra-chain scores provides a more accurate and comprehensive evaluation of protein-protein docking models.
reason: After completing the main experiments for protein complexes (e_14), a conclusive analysis is the critical next step. This event serves to interpret the comprehensive results, validate that the revised method (e_13) meets the expanded research goals (e_09), and produce the key findings for the paper's results section.

subject: Biology
description: Consolidated the findings from both single-chain and protein-complex experiments into a unified manuscript draft. The Methods and Results sections were updated to integrate the work on protein complexes, and initial drafts of the Introduction and Discussion sections were written to frame the overall contribution of the new composite metric.
reason: With the successful analysis of the main experiments for both single-chain proteins (e_07) and protein complexes (e_15), the core research is complete. The next necessary step is to synthesize all findings into a single, cohesive manuscript, integrating the separate paper paragraphs and results into a full draft ready for internal review.

subject: Biology
description: The full manuscript draft underwent internal review by co-authors. Based on feedback, the Introduction was revised to better motivate the problem, and the Discussion was expanded to more clearly articulate the metric's limitations and suggest future work. All figures and tables were also polished for clarity.
reason: After consolidating all findings into a first draft (e_16), a critical step is to refine the manuscript based on internal feedback. This revision process improves the paper's narrative, clarity, and overall quality, preparing it for submission to a journal.

subject: Biology
description: The manuscript, having undergone internal review and revision, is considered complete. The project concludes with the submission of the paper to a peer-reviewed journal, presenting the novel composite metric for evaluating both single-chain protein structures and protein-protein complexes.
reason: The project has reached a natural conclusion. The initial research goals have been met and expanded upon, the methodological scheme has been iteratively refined and validated through main experiments, and a complete manuscript has been drafted and internally reviewed (e_17). With conclusive results and a polished paper, the project's active research phase is complete.
